text: The surgeon attempts to elicit the rectal prolapse by instructing the patient to cough, observing any visible protrusion or movement of the rectal tissue.

The surgeon prepares to perform a staple transanal rectal resection for complete rectal prolapse, using a circular staple device. The stay sutures are placed on each side, and the stapler is closed with the orange mark in the green firing zone. The surgeon checks the vaginal wall to ensure it is not entrapped, confirming it is free before firing the stapler. Due to the extent of the prolapse, the device is opened and removed.

The surgeon opens the circular stapler device and carefully removes it under direct vision, revealing the full-thickness section of the rectum and parietal fat. The bridge is broken, and the scales are slowly removed, ensuring the posterior rectum or lumen remains intact and protected. The surgeon then targets the prolapsed rectum at the nine o'clock position using the circular stapler device.

The surgeon positions the stapler on the scale to perform a full-thickness resection of the rectum. The resection reveals parietal fat surrounding the specimen, indicating a complete resection. The surgeon then places a stay suture at the six o'clock position, targeting the highest point of the prolapse. The lateral residual prolapse is removed, and the full-thickness resection is confirmed with visible parietal fat.

The surgeon addresses the residual prolapse component on the patient's left side, using a stay suture and a sixth stapler for full-thickness excision. The parietal fat is visible, confirming the resection. The surgeon checks for any remaining prolapse by inserting a gauze piece, which moves freely without obstruction, indicating no residual prolapse. The patient is asked to cough and strain, but no prolapse is observed.
